result.markdown:
--- title: "A first Regression with PyFixest" aliases: - /quickstart.html format: html: html-table-processing: none toc: true toc-title: "On this page" toc-location: left --- `PyFixest` is a Python package for fast high-dimensional fixed effects regression. The package aims to mimic the syntax and functionality of [Laurent Bergé's](https://sites.google.com/site/laurentrberge/) formidable [fixest](https://github.com/lrberge/fixest) package as closely as Python allows: if you know `fixest` well, the goal is that you won't have to read the docs to get started! In particular, this means that all of `fixest's` defaults are mirrored by `PyFixest`. ## Installation You can install `pyfixest` from [PyPi](https://pypi.org/project/pyfixest/) https://pypi.org/project/pyfixest/ via ```bash pip install -U pyfixest ``` ## A First Regression We want to estimate the **causal returns of education on earnings** via a twin study. In this notebook, we focus on `PyFixest` estimation functionality and syntax. For details on the question at hand, please take a look at the [OLS with Fixed Effects](tutorials/ols-fixed-effects.qmd) vignette. In a first step, we load a twin-study style data set: ::: {#9c73f531 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pyfixest as pf twins = pf.get_twin_data(N_pairs=500, seed=42) twins.head() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ```{=html}
twin_pair_id twin_id ability educ age experience log_wage
0 1 1 0.304717 14.880083 38.0 17.119917 3.241823
1 1 2 0.304717 13.942729 49.0 29.057271 3.379130
2 2 1 -1.039984 10.041047 33.0 16.958953 2.303006
3 2 2 -1.039984 8.475001 32.0 17.524999 2.057258
4 3 1 0.750451 8.000000 35.0 21.000000 3.449381
``` ::: ::: `PyFixest`'s core estimation function is called `feols()`. As a bare minimum, you need to pass a `pandas` or `polars` data frame and a Wilkinson formula. We first estimate a naive OLS regression that is likely biased by endogeneity (unobserved ability). Then we estimate a twin fixed-effects model. In both models, we cluster standard errors at the twin-pair level. ::: {#c918ae1b .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} fit_naive = pf.feols("log_wage ~ educ + experience", data=twins, vcov={"CRV1": "twin_pair_id"}) fit_fe = pf.feols("log_wage ~ educ + experience | twin_pair_id", data=twins, vcov={"CRV1": "twin_pair_id"}) ``` ::: We compare both specifications side by side via `etable()`. ::: {#8482bc3d .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} pf.etable( [fit_naive, fit_fe], labels={"log_wage": "Log Hourly Wage", "educ": "Years of Education", "experience": "Experience"}, felabels={"twin_pair_id": "Twin Pair FE"}, caption="Returns to Education: Naive OLS vs Twin Fixed Effects", ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=6} ```{=html}
Returns to Education: Naive OLS vs Twin Fixed Effects
Log Hourly Wage
(1) (2)
coef
Years of Education 0.114***
(0.007)		 0.088***
(0.007)
Experience 0.019***
(0.002)		 0.02***
(0.002)
Intercept 1.113***
(0.098)
fe
Twin Pair FE - x
stats
Observations 1,000 1,000
R2 0.283 0.801
Significance levels: * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001. Format of coefficient cell: Coefficient (Std. Error)
``` ::: ::: ## Other relevant Features After the initial regression, we recommend jumping directly to focused tutorials: - [Formula Syntax](tutorials/formula-syntax.qmd): one-, two-, and three-part formulas, `i()`, and multiple-estimation operators. - [Standard Errors & Inference](tutorials/standard-errors.qmd): heteroskedastic, clustered, and resampling-based inference. - [Instrumental Variables](tutorials/instrumental-variables.qmd): IV estimation and diagnostics. - [Poisson & GLMs](tutorials/poisson-glm.qmd): count-data models and generalized linear models. - [Quantile Regression](tutorials/quantile-regression.qmd): distributional effects beyond the mean. ## Where to Go Next | Tutorial | Description | |---|---| | [Formula Syntax](tutorials/formula-syntax.qmd) | Full formula language: FE, IV, `C()`, `i()`, and multiple-estimation syntax | | [OLS with Fixed Effects](tutorials/ols-fixed-effects.qmd) | Twin studies, worker-firm panels, demeaning intuition | | [Standard Errors & Inference](tutorials/standard-errors.qmd) | HC, cluster-robust, wild bootstrap, randomization inference | | [Instrumental Variables](tutorials/instrumental-variables.qmd) | IV estimation, diagnostics, first-stage F-statistics | | [Poisson & GLMs](tutorials/poisson-glm.qmd) | Count data models, logit, probit | | [Multiple Estimation](tutorials/multiple-estimation.qmd) | `sw`, `csw`, split samples, multi-dep-var syntax | | [Regression Tables](tutorials/regression-tables.qmd) | Publication-ready tables with Great Tables | | [Difference-in-Differences](tutorials/difference-in-differences.qmd) | TWFE, Gardner's DID2S, event studies | | [Quantile Regression](tutorials/quantile-regression.qmd) | Interior-point quantile regression | You can browse all tutorials in the [Tutorial Gallery](tutorials/index.qmd), or see the [How-To Guides](how-to/marginaleffects.qmd) for task-oriented recipes. The [Function Reference](reference/index.qmd) has all details around functions and their arguments, classes, methods, and attributes.
